text: The surgeon performs a low anterior resection with a complete total mesorectal excision (TME) and a sewn coloanal anastomosis for a rectosigmoid cancer. The tumor is located just above the peritoneal reflection. The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery distal to the left colic artery take-off and performs lateral dissection, dropping the gonadal vessels off the mesocolon. The TME dissection proceeds posteriorly and circumferentially around the rectum, maintaining the dissection within the 'holy plane' to achieve an adequate distal margin. The surgeon carefully navigates around the anterior structures, avoiding the vagina, and addresses the folded tissue from prior gynecological surgery.

The surgeon begins the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection posteriorly, moving along the right side in a circumferential manner, maintaining the dissection within the 'holy plane' to achieve an adequate distal margin. The dissection continues down the left side, addressing folds from prior gynecological surgery, and progresses anteriorly, carefully avoiding the vagina in the deep cul-de-sac. The mesorectum and rectum beneath the peritoneal reflection are notably short, approximately three centimeters.

The surgeon identifies the tumor and ensures an adequate margin is achieved. The vagina is released from the mesorectum, completing the posterior dissection down to the levator floor. The surgeon circumferentially dissects, using the camera to visualize deeper areas and avoid the vagina. Below the lower extent of the levators, the surgeon uses a vessel sealer for hemostasis and to prevent burning the rectum. At the floor, the remaining mesorectum is minimal, focusing on muscular attachments of the levators to the rectal wall. The proximal mesentery is taken, and the surgeon prepares to staple proximally.

The surgeon identifies the tumor and ensures an adequate margin while releasing the vagina from the mesorectum. The posterior dissection is completed down to the levator floor, with circumferential dissection aided by the camera for deeper visualization. The surgeon reaches below the lower extent of the levators anteriorly and uses a vessel sealer for hemostasis, avoiding inadvertent burns to the rectum. At the floor, the remaining mesorectum is minimal, consisting mainly of muscular attachments of the levators to the rectal wall. The proximal mesentery has been taken, and the surgeon prepares to staple proximally.

The surgeon observes good perfusion and staples the distal end. In a wide open female pelvis, a sewn side-to-end Baker anastomosis is performed from the side of the proximal colon to the staple line on the rectum. The surgeon notes a dusky appearance at the corner of the proximal bowel and uses ICG to assess blood supply, revealing a lack of enhancement in the area, prompting a revision of the anastomosis.

The surgeon assesses the perfusion of the bowel and staples the distal end. In a wide open female pelvis with excellent visualization, a sewn side-to-end Baker anastomosis is performed from the side of the proximal colon to the staple line on the rectum. The surgeon notes a dusky appearance at the corner of the proximal bowel and uses ICG to confirm that the area does not enhance, prompting a revision of the anastomosis.

The surgeon takes down the initial staple line and resects a few more centimeters to establish a new proximal margin. The surgeon then re-sutures the original posterior suture line, connecting the side of the colon to the end of the rectum.

The surgeon takes down the initial staple line suture line and resects a few more centimeters to establish a new proximal margin. The original posterior suture line is then re-sutured, connecting the side of the colon to the end of the rectum.

The surgeon completes the posterior outer row of sutures and makes an enterotomy on the anterior rectum, cleaning out stool. The interrectal ring is visible, along with hemorrhoids through the anterior rectal opening. A proximal colotomy is made, and the surgeon begins the inner posterior row of sutures. ICG is used to assess perfusion, with improved results noted.

The surgeon completes the posterior outer row of sutures and makes an enterotomy on the anterior rectum, cleaning out stool from the rectum. The hemorrhoids are visible through the opening of the anterior rectum. A proximal colotomy is made, and the inner posterior row of sutures is started. The surgeon uses ICG to check perfusion, expressing satisfaction with the results.

The surgeon observes red bleeding, indicating good perfusion, while performing a complex anastomosis. The stability of the platform and instrument reticulation facilitate precise suture placement. The surgeon transitions to imbricating the inner and anterior rows, ensuring sutures do not meet at the end. The anterior lip of the rectum is visible, positioned low near the perineal body with minimal clearance.

The surgeon observes red bleeding, indicating good perfusion, while performing a complex anastomosis using a stable platform and reticulated instruments. The surgeon avoids using an EEA stapler, preferring to sew the side before transitioning to the anterior. Sutures are placed precisely to avoid closing the posterior row too far. The surgeon imbricates the inner and anterior row, ensuring sutures do not meet at the end, and notes the low position on the anterior lip of the rectum near the perineal body with minimal clearance.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and converts to laparoscopy to perform a leak test. The anastomosis is filled with gas, showing a widely patent and distended structure without any bubbles, indicating no leakage.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis and converts to laparoscopy to perform a leak test. The bowel is filled with gas, showing a widely patent and distended anastomosis without any bubbles, indicating no leaks.
